content: NPH treatment is surgical CSF diversion. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt is standard treatment; lumboperitoneal shunt alternative. Shunt types: programmable valves allow non-invasive pressure adjustment. Outcomes: gait improves most (60-80%), urinary symptoms (50-70%), cognition least responsive (30-50%). Best outcomes when treated early; gait disturbance duration <2 years predicts better response. Preoperative predictors of shunt response: large-volume lumbar drain (removes 30-50 mL CSF over 2-3 days) with objective gait/cognitive testing before and after; improvement predicts shunt success. Tap test (single large-volume LP 30-50 mL) less sensitive but more convenient. Complications: subdural hematoma/hygroma (10-15%, from brain sagging—adjust valve pressure higher), shunt infection (3-5%), shunt malfunction/obstruction requiring revision (30% within 1 year), overdrainage (orthostatic headache). Post-operative care: gradual mobilization, monitor for complications, valve pressure adjustments based on clinical response and imaging.
